perturbation.original_text: You agree to pay us, within ten (10) days of our written request (which is accompanied by reasonable substantiating material), any amounts that we have paid, that we have become obligated to pay, and/or that we choose to pay on your behalf.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to pay us, within ten (10) days of our written request (which is accompanied by reasonable substantiating material), any amounts that we have paid, that we have become obligated to pay, and/or that we choose to pay on your behalf. 3.13 Confirmation of Performance. After we have performed our pre-opening obligations to you under this Agreement, we may ask that you execute and deliver to us a confirmation (the "Confirmation of Performance"), in a form we reasonably request, confirming that we have performed those obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the obligation to pay amounts paid on the franchisee's behalf to be near a clause discussing "Confirmation of Performance" makes the franchisee's responsibility to pay for the original payment obscure.
perturbation.law_explanation: TILA requires clear and conspicuous disclosure of finance charges and related terms. Hiding a financial obligation within a section about performance confirmation obscures this disclosure, violating the principle of clear presentation of financial terms.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1638 - Transactions other than under an open end credit plan | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1638 - Transactions other than under an open end credit plan U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Required disclosures by creditor For each consumer credit transaction other than under an open end credit plan, the creditor shall disclose each of the following items, to the extent applicable: (1) The identity of the creditor required to make disclosure. (2) (A) The “amount financed”, using that term, which shall be the amount of credit of which the consumer has actual use. This amount shall be computed as follows, but the computations need not be disclosed and shall not be disclosed with the disclosures conspicuously segregated in accordance with subsection (b)(1): (i) take the principal amount of the loan or the cash price less downpayment and trade-in; (ii) add any charges which are not part of the finance charge or of the principal amount of the loan and which are financed by the consumer, including the cost of any items excluded from the finance charge pursuant to section 1605 of this title ; and (iii) subtract any charges which are part of the finance charge but which will be paid by the consumer before or at the time of the consummation of the transaction, or have been withheld from the proceeds of the credit. (B) In conjunction with the disclosure of the amount financed, a creditor shall provide a statement of the consumer’s right to obtain, upon a written request, a written itemization of the amount financed . The statement shall include spaces for a “yes” and “no” indication to be initialed by the consumer to indicate whether the consumer wants a written itemization of the amount financed . Upon receiving an affirmative indication, the creditor shall provide, at the time other disclosures are required to be furnished, a written
perturbation.justification: The changed text presents a structural flaw by relocating a financial obligation into a section concerning 'Confirmation of Performance.' This obscures the franchisee's responsibility to pay amounts on their behalf, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation correctly asserts that this violates TILA by not clearly disclosing finance charges. While the scraped snippets of 15 U.S. Code § 1638 do not directly mention 'clear and conspicuous disclosure,' they broadly mandate that creditors disclose applicable financial items for consumer credit transactions. By placing this financial obligation within a section about performance, the modification effectively hides this duty and contradicts the intent of the law to ensure clear disclosure, creating a situation that a court would likely find non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: The term "Operating Codes" means applicable federal, state, and local laws, codes, ordinances, and/or regulations that apply to the Services, products, construction and design of the Franchised Business and other aspects of operating the Franchised Business, including the ADA. You must furnish to us, within three (3) days of your receipt, a copy of all inspection reports, warnings, citations, certificates, and/or ratings resulting from inspections conducted by any federal, state or municipal agency with jurisdiction over the Franchised Business.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Operating Codes" means applicable federal, state, and local laws, codes, ordinances, and/or regulations that apply to the Services, products, construction and design of the Franchised Business and other aspects of operating the Franchised Business, including the ADA. We are committed to working to attain optimal performance of Goosehead Businesses with respect to environmental, sustainability, and energy performance. You will have the sole responsibility to fully and faithfully comply with any Operating Codes, and we will not review whether you are in compliance with any Operating Codes.
perturbation.explanation: Hiding the obligation to furnish reports within the definition of 'Operating Codes' and nearby a statement about environmental commitment obscures its importance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Compliance: By separating the disclosure to furnish the documents away from the direct reference to the ADA creates uncertainty regarding compliance with the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the franchisee to furnish inspection reports, warnings, citations, certificates, and ratings to the franchisor within three days. The changed text removes this obligation. The perturbation explanation indicates this obligation is hidden within the definition of 'Operating Codes,' obscuring its importance. This creates a structural flaw because a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section. While the scraped snippets focus on ADA compliance broadly, the absence of a procedure to collect the reports weakens compliance by potentially impeding the franchisor's ability to monitor and ensure ADA adherence. Therefore, the change weakens or removes a core legal obligation, thus creating a contradiction.
